context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a philosophy question about when it’s morally okay to kill one person to save five. I’m pretty confused about how act consequentialism handles the trolley-type cases, so I could use help sorting that out.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—start by separating the task into 4 parts. What do you think act consequentialism says makes an act right or wrong in the first place?

Once you state that rule in your own words, ask: in a case where 1 dies and 5 live, what feature of the outcome seems morally decisive for an act consequentialist?

Try answering just that first, in 2–3 sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in tutor role, keep the response brief, and use Socratic questions that move directly toward the assignment. I will not provide conclusions about act consequentialism or the trolley cases. Instead, I will help the student distinguish the assignment’s parts: the general question, the act consequentialist view, application to cases, and critical evaluation. This supports guided discovery and avoids giving a ready-made answer.

exchanges.student: I think act consequentialism says an act is right if that specific act leads to the best overall consequences compared with the alternatives. So in a case where 1 dies and 5 live, the decisive feature seems to be that fewer people die overall, so saving the five would usually look morally better.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now test your rule against the cases.

If act consequentialism cares only about which available act has the best outcome, would it treat Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases differently in principle—or the same? What matters to it besides the final balance of lives lost and saved?

Pick one case and walk it through.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's progress briefly, then push them to apply their stated principle to the lecture cases without supplying the applications myself. The questions will direct them to test whether act consequentialism treats structurally different cases the same, which is central to the assignment. This keeps the interaction bite-sized, Socratic, and focused on the philosophy problem.

exchanges.student: I think it would treat them basically the same in principle, unless there are other consequences I’m forgetting. If all it cares about is which act produces the best overall outcome, then what seems to matter most is the total result, like one death instead of five, not whether it’s a switch case or a more personal killing case.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the pressure point.

So now ask the harder question: do you think that’s the right result? Should morality ignore distinctions like using someone as a means, intending harm, or direct personal violence if the numbers come out better?

Which difference between the cases seems morally important to you, if any?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's emerging understanding and now guide them toward the evaluative part of the assignment: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right. I will not state objections for them, but I will ask a targeted contrast question about moral distinctions like intention, means, and personal force, since those are likely relevant to trolley-style cases. This advances the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I don’t think that’s fully right. It seems morally important whether the person is just foreseen to die versus deliberately used as a means to save the five, and also whether I’m directly attacking someone. I’m not totally sure how to state that cleanly, though.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the core issue. Don’t try to make it elegant yet—make it precise.

Ask yourself: is it ever okay when the one person’s death is part of the plan for saving the five, versus a side effect of carrying out the plan? And does “directly attacking” mark a real moral boundary or just an emotional reaction?

Try drafting a rough rule in your own words, even if it’s ugly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's key insight and guide them to formulate a general principle themselves. I will avoid supplying doctrine names or polished wording, since the student must do the writing. My questions will narrow the distinction into a usable test for the cases and for the final 'right general account' part of the assignment.

exchanges.student: My rough rule is that it can be okay to save the five if the one person’s death is a side effect, but not if their death is part of the plan or the tool for saving the five. I think “directly attacking” might matter only if it shows that I’m using the person as a means, but I’m not sure if directness by itself is a real moral boundary.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong draft. Now stress-test it.

For each lecture case, ask: is the one person’s death a side effect, or is it being used as part of the rescue plan? Your rule should sort the cases into those two buckets.

Take two cases you remember best and classify them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has a promising principle. I should help them stress-test it against the lecture cases, since the assignment requires application and evaluation. I will not map cases to outcomes for them; instead I will ask them to sort the cases using their own rule. This maintains guided discovery, keeps it concise, and moves directly toward a workable essay structure.

exchanges.student: I think in a standard trolley switch case, the one person’s death looks more like a side effect, because the plan is to redirect the trolley away from the five, even though I know the one will die. But in a case where the person has to be harmed as the way of stopping the threat, that seems like using them as part of the plan, so my rule would say that’s not okay.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now connect that back to the theory.

So where exactly does your view break from act consequentialism? Is the disagreement that consequences are not the only thing that matter?

And one more step: what’s the strongest objection to your rule? Try naming one problem case or ambiguity.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's application and now push them to connect it explicitly back to act consequentialism and the final argumentative structure. I will not provide a final thesis; I will ask them to articulate the disagreement and identify a possible weakness in their own rule. This helps them build a balanced philosophical response without handing them the content.

exchanges.student: I think my view breaks from act consequentialism because I’m saying consequences aren’t the only thing that matter; the way the harm happens matters too, especially whether someone is used as a means. The strongest objection is probably that the side effect vs means distinction can get fuzzy, and I’m not always sure how to tell which description of the plan is the right one.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the real philosophical tension, and it’s a good one.

Now put the bigger picture together: can you outline your response in 4 parts—act consequentialism’s rule, how it handles the cases, why that seems wrong in some cases, and your alternative rule plus its main difficulty?

Write that outline in your own words. Keep it rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student's progress and direct them to synthesize their ideas into a compact argumentative structure, since they now have the key components: the act consequentialist principle, application to cases, disagreement, and an objection. I will avoid writing the answer for them or suggesting polished language. I will use a final Socratic prompt that requires them to assemble their own paragraph-level plan.

exchanges.student:
Okay, I’d outline it like this:

1. Act consequentialism says an act is right if it has the best overall consequences among the available options.  
2. So it would treat the lecture cases mainly by comparing outcomes, which means if killing one saves five, it will usually say that’s the right act no matter the type of case.  
3. That seems wrong in at least some cases because morality probably shouldn’t ignore the difference between harming someone as a side effect and using them as a means.  
4. My alternative rule is that it’s only okay to kill one to save five when the death is a foreseen side effect rather than part of the plan, but the hard part is that this distinction can be blurry.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s a workable backbone.

Now make it philosophical, not just descriptive: what is your reason for thinking “using as a means” matters morally? And how would you defend your rule against the objection that blurry cases weaken it?

Answer those two questions, and you’ll have the core of your paper.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has assembled the core structure. I should acknowledge progress, avoid polishing or rewriting, and use a final Socratic push to deepen philosophical rigor by asking for support and counterargument handling. This keeps the response short, focused on the assignment, and avoids giving a complete solution while moving them toward a stronger draft.

exchanges.student: I think using someone as a means matters because it treats that person more like a tool than as someone with their own moral status, which seems like a failure to respect them. For the blurry-case objection, I guess I’d say some moral distinctions can be hard to apply without being fake, so vagueness doesn’t automatically show the rule is wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s strong. You now have an actual argument, not just a reaction.

Final step: can you state your overall conclusion clearly—when is it morally okay, when is it not, and why doesn’t act consequentialism capture that boundary?

Write it as your own short thesis plus 2–3 supporting claims.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's substantive progress and prompt one final step of self-generated integration. I will not rewrite or polish their argument, and I will keep the response concise. My question will push them to connect respect for persons to case judgments and to state a limited, defensible conclusion, which advances the assignment without giving a full solution.

exchanges.student: My thesis is that it is morally okay to kill one person to save five only when the person’s death is a foreseen side effect of saving the five, not when that person is intentionally used as a means to do it. Act consequentialism misses this boundary because it looks only at maximizing good outcomes, so it treats very different kinds of killing as morally the same if the numbers work out. I think that’s wrong because how a person is treated matters morally, and using someone as a tool fails to respect their status as a person.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s a clear, defensible position.

Last check: can you now map that thesis onto 2–3 specific lecture cases and show which side of your boundary each falls on? Then ask yourself: is there any case where your “side effect” rule gives a result you’re uneasy about?

Do that, and your draft has real substance.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis and core support. I should acknowledge that progress without grading, avoid rewriting or polishing their prose, and push one last step of philosophical development: applying the thesis back to the lecture cases and testing it against a possible objection. This follows the Socratic method, keeps the answer bite-sized, and does not provide a complete solution.

deductions.reason: After the student improved from rough drafts to a clear thesis (Turn 10), the tutor moved on to the next task without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or reinforcing the method choices that led to the improvement.

After the student improved from rough drafts to a clear thesis (Turn 10), the tutor moved on to the next task without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or reinforcing the method choices that led to the improvement.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted a well-structured Socratic dialogue throughout the conversation, consistently prompting the student to generate ideas, test rules, and build toward a thesis without giving away answers. Scaffolding was progressive and responsive to student progress. The only clear deduction is for failing to prompt meta-reflection after the student's successful improvement in Turn 10, missing an opportunity to reinforce the reasoning process that led to the strong final thesis.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate key ideas before confirming or probing them. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor built on each student response, acknowledged correct progress, and used diagnostic questions before advancing. Full marks. 1.3: The tutor explained its scaffolding approach implicitly through structured prompts and gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'make it precise, not elegant'), but after the student produced a strong thesis in Turn 10, the tutor moved directly to the next task without asking the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking—triggering 1.3.C.a for 2 points deducted. Dialogue quality and communication quality were both strong: responses were bite-sized, interactive, and consistently on-task with a supportive tone.
